content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 2,
1992
Carolyn Pateman
Director
Volunteers for a Valley
Center High School.
27930 valley Center Rd.
Valley Center,
CA 92082
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-285
Dear Ms.
Pateman:
You have requested advice on behalf of Volunteers for a
Valley Center High School
(“Volunteers”)
concerning the lobbyin
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) 1
QUESTION
What are the registration and reporting obligations of
Volunteers under the lobbying disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act pursuant to their support of or opposition to
several ballot and bond measures?
CONCLUSION
Volunteers has no registration or reporting obligations under
the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Act but it may have
reporting obligations under the campaign reporting provisions.
FACTS
You are the director of Volunteers,
a tax—exempt nonprofit
corporation which is formed for the specific purpose of increasing
support for the building of a new high school in Valley Center.
In addition, Volunteers intends to support or oppose state and
local ballot and bond measures.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92—285
Page
2
ANALYSIS
Regulation 18239 defines lobbyist as “any person who,
for
compensation,
engages in direct communication, other than
administrative testimony, with a qualifying official for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action..
.
Section 82037 provides that “legislative action” means the
drafting,
introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or
defeat of any bill, resolution,
amendment, report,
nomination or
other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any
committee,
subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof,
or by
a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official
capacity.
“Legislative action” also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill.
“Administrative action” means attempting to influence any
state agency in regard to its regulations or policies.
(Section
82002.)
It does not appear that Volunteers is engaged in any activity
which is defined as lobbying under the Act.
However,
your
activities may fall under the campaign disclosure provisions of
the Act.
Section 82013 defines “committee” as:
[A]ny person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly does any of the following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more in a calendar year.
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more in a calendar year;
or
(c)
Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees.
A person
2
! who receives money for “political purposes” has
received “contributions,” and must register and file disclosure
reports as a “recipient committee” under Section 82013(a) if
$1,000 or more is received during a calendar year.
Section 82015
and Regulation 18215 define “contribution.”
Regulation 18215 defines
“a payment for political purposes,”
in relevant part,
as a payment:
(1)
For the purpose of influencing or attempting
2/
Section 82047
includes committees and any type of
organization or group of persons acting in concert.


Our File No.
A-92-285
Page
3
to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates,
or the
qualification or passage of any measure....
Emphasis added.
If Volunteers receives $1,000 or more in a calendar year for
the purpose of supporting or opposing the qualification or passage
of state or local ballot measures,
including bond measures, it
will qualify as a committee and incur campaign disclosure
V
obligations.
Section 84101(a)
requires a committee qualified
under Section 82013(a)
to file a Statement of Organization
(Form
410)
with the Secretary of State within 10 days of receiving
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year.
If the committee qualifies under Section 82013(a), it will
also be required to file periodic campaign reports
(Form 420 or
450).
Because Volunteers is supporting one state measure and two
local ballot or bond measures, it will be a state general purpose
recipient committee.
(Section 82027.5.)
Volunteers must file an
original and one copy of its campaign statements with the
Secretary of State.
In addition, it must file two copies with the
Registrar of Voters for Los Angeles County,
two copies with the
Registrar of Voters for the City and County of San Francisco,
and
two copies with the committee’s county of domicile if it is not
Los Angeles or San Francisco.
Once Volunteers qualifies as
a committee, it must file semi
annual statements until it terminates.
Semi—annual statements are-
due no later than July
31 for the period ending June 30,
and no
later than January 31 for the period ending December 31.
(Section
84200.)
It may also be required to file pre-election statements
depending on whether it makes contributions or independent
expenditures totalling $500 or more within certain pre—election
filing periods.
The pre-election deadlines vary depending on the
date of the election.
The Secretary of State can provide you with
the appropriate forms, filing dates,
and the Commission’s campaign
information manual
(Manual
C——Campaign Manual for General Purpose
Committees).
If you have additional questions,
please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
WajJne P.
Imberi
Po’litical Reform Consultant

sections.question:
What are the registration and reporting obligations of
Volunteers under the lobbying disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act pursuant to their support of or opposition to
several ballot and bond measures?
sections.conclusion:
Volunteers has no registration or reporting obligations under
the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Act but it may have
reporting obligations under the campaign reporting provisions.

2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No.
A-92—285

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the registration and reporting obligations of
Volunteers under the lobbying disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act pursuant to their support of or opposition to
several ballot and bond measures?

CONCLUSION: Volunteers has no registration or reporting obligations under
the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Act but it may have
reporting obligations under the campaign reporting provisions.
